text: The surgeon begins the procedure on the left side, identifying the left ureter and using a lateral to medial approach to dissect the descending colon towards the splenic flexure. The omentum is separated from the distal transverse colon at the splenic flexure, with options to either remove it from the distal transverse colon or enter the lesser sac to take the omentum with the splenic flexure, depending on the patient's body habitus.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the splenic flexure and shifts focus to the right colon. The right ureter is identified, and a lateral to medial dissection is performed, scoring the peritoneum around the base of the cecum and terminal ileum to enter the retroperitoneal plane. Using a BOVI cautery device, the surgeon dissects along the right lateral peritoneal reflection, exposing the right retroperitoneum, including the right ureter, duodenum, and inferior vena cava. The dissection progresses towards the hepatic flexure, ensuring the plane turns medially to avoid the right kidney. The final mobilization involves dividing the medial attachments of the terminal ileum up to the duodenum and elevating the hepatocolic attachments at the hepatic flexure.

The surgeon mobilizes the hepatic flexure below the umbilicus, indicating adequate mobilization of the right colon for extraction. Attention is then turned to the pelvis, where the left ureter is visible in the left lower quadrant. The surgeon scores the left pararectal tissues to enter the presacral space, elevating the rectum using simple cautery in a bloodless plane. The dissection proceeds posteriorly, maintaining tension on the rectum, and continues along the left lateral pararectal attachments before moving to the right side of the rectum.

The surgeon identifies the right ureter in the right lower quadrant and scores the right pararectal tissues to enter the presacral space, continuing dissection with tension on the tissues. In a female patient, the uterus is elevated using a Keith needle and transcutaneous stitches to avoid obstruction. The posterior wall of the vagina is carefully delineated to prevent damage. Traction is applied to the rectum using a stapler through the right lower quadrant port, with assistance from the perineum. The staple line is visible coning down into the anal canal, leaving minimal mucosa in the proximal half.

The surgeon exteriorizes the entire colon and rectum through a peri-umbilical incision, positioning them as midline structures. The small bowel mesentery is accessed beneath the incision, facilitating the creation of an ileoanal pouch with adequate length. The small bowel is exteriorized to check for signs of Crohn's disease in the patient with presumed ulcerative colitis.

The surgeon makes final incisions using a peri-umbilical extraction site and two five-millimeter ports at the ileostomy, with a suprapubic port site used to bring out a drain. The patient's abdomen is shown post-ileostomy closure, demonstrating a cosmetic result.

The surgeon extracts the entire colon and rectum through the ileostomy site, avoiding the need for a peri-umbilical extraction incision. Despite the bulky nature of the rectum due to inflammation, it is successfully exteriorized through the standard ileostomy site. The surgeon then proceeds to create a J-pouch, ensuring it reaches down to the pubis.
